title: cors() with origin: true and credentials — reflects any caller's origin, leaking authenticated responses cross-site

params.passDetail: cors() pins origin to an explicit trusted value (not the reflect-everything `true`) — cross-origin credentialed access is limited to that allow-list.
params.failDetail: cors() is called with origin: true, which REFLECTS whatever Origin the request carries back in Access-Control-Allow-Origin. With credentials: true this defeats the browser's `*`-plus-credentials guard: every site the victim visits can make credentialed cross-origin calls and read the authenticated responses. Pin origin to an explicit allow-list (string, array, or a validating function), never `true`.
params.absentCallDetail: No cors() call in this scope; this rule does not apply here.
params.absentArgDetail: cors() does not pass an origin literal; verify the resolved origin is a trusted allow-list, not a blanket reflect.

provenance.note: Observed in idurar/idurar-erp-crm (cors({ origin: true, credentials: true })). origin: true reads as 'just make CORS work' but reflects every origin; combined with credentials it is functionally an open credentialed CORS policy. Distinct from origin: "*", which browsers reject when credentials are set — `true` slips past that guard.
